chunk_summary: Hospitalization thresholds and mental health referral for ARFID involve assessing malnutrition severity, medical risks, and psychosocial impairment, with explicit criteria for admission and outpatient management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic criteria and differentiation of ARFID from anorexia nervosa.
Identify medical risk factors and hospitalization thresholds for ARFID patients.
Learn outpatient management strategies, including exposure-based and CBT-oriented approaches.
Recognize psychosocial impairments and their role in ARFID screening.
key_facts: ARFID is characterized by severe restriction causing weight loss, faltering growth, nutritional deficiency, or psychosocial impairment, without body-image concerns typical of anorexia nervosa.

Hospitalization is recommended for severe malnutrition (<75% median BMI), dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, abnormal ECG (e.g., prolonged QTc), bradycardia (<50/min daytime or <45/min nighttime), hypotension (<90/45 mmHg), hypothermia, or orthostatic instability.
Medical risk assessment includes vital signs, hydration status, percent median BMI, BMI Z score, and rate of weight loss.
Outpatient management focuses on exposure-based, anxiety-focused work, often using CBT-oriented approaches.
Screening involves assessing psychosocial impairment, maintaining mechanisms (e.g., sensory sensitivity, fear of aversive consequences), and ruling out anorexia-spectrum illness.
reference_quote: SAHM guidance supports admission when there is severe malnutrition (for example below 75% median BMI), dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, abnormal ECG including prolonged QTc, severe bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, or orthostatic instability.
